Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit chaotic, but I made it work. I usually carved out time in the evenings after my part-time job, and I'd dedicate bigger chunks on weekends when I could. I kept a notebook where I'd write down what needed to be done each week, and I'd tick things off as I went. With 28 hours a week of studying, I had to be strategic about what I prioritized since I couldn't afford to waste time.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I tried to keep some consistency, like Sunday afternoons for reading and Tuesday evenings for working through course materials, but deadlines would throw everything off. When assignments were coming up, I'd have to shift everything around and sometimes pull longer sessions. It wasn't ideal, but I learned to adapt quickly to whatever was most urgent.
Q3: Usually it was either checking if new materials had been posted or seeing if there were discussion posts I needed to respond to. Sometimes I'd get a notification about an upcoming deadline and that would prompt me to log in and figure out what I still needed to do. The peer support group I was part of would also message about meeting up online, so that got me logging in more regularly than I might have otherwise.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became automatic to check the LMS in the morning with my coffee before heading out. The peer support connections really helped with that because I wanted to see what others were discussing and contribute when I could. It felt less like a chore once I started recognizing names and building those relationships through the discussion boards. Having that social element made logging in feel more natural.
Q5: The reading materials and lecture notes were absolutely essential for me since I learn best through text. I'd often print things out or copy them into my own documents so I could highlight and annotate. The discussion forums were surprisingly helpful too because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me different perspectives. The video content was useful but I found myself taking notes from it rather than rewatching.
Q6: Some of the supplementary videos I'd skip or only skim because they took so much time and I could usually get the same information faster from the written materials. A few of the optional quizzes I ignored when time was tight because they weren't graded. I know I probably missed out on some learning there, but with limited resources and time, I had to make tough choices about where to focus my energy.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to what I'd be assessed on or if it genuinely helped me understand something I was struggling with. The peer discussions felt valuable because they helped me process ideas and I wasn't doing it alone. If something seemed like busy work or repetitive, I'd deprioritize it. I needed to see clear value given how stretched I already was.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. Required ones got done no matter what because they affected my grade, but optional ones I'd only tackle if I had extra time or was genuinely curious. Sometimes I'd feel guilty skipping optional stuff because I knew it could help, but I had to be realistic about my capacity. The required activities had this weight to them that optional ones just didn't carry.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could study during my commute or breaks at work. I also used a lot of external YouTube videos and free resources online when the course materials weren't clicking for me. My handwritten notes were probably where most of my actual learning happened, but none of that would show up anywhere.
Q10: There were many times I'd be sitting with printed materials or my notebook, working through problems and concepts for hours, and none of that registered online. I'd also study with peers from my support group in person at the library, discussing course content and quizzing each other. Those sessions were incredibly valuable for my learning but completely invisible to the system. My best thinking often happened away from a screen.
Q11: All the time, actually. I'd download lecture slides or readings once, then work from those files for days or weeks without going back to the LMS. With my limited data plan and sometimes spotty internet, it made more sense to grab everything at once and study offline. I'd only go back online when I needed something new or had to submit work. It was just more practical for my situation.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when assignments from multiple courses piled up at once. My stress levels would spike and I'd feel overwhelmed, even though I tried to stay resilient. There were also times when work demanded more hours and I had to choose between earning money and studying. Those weeks my engagement dropped because I was just trying to survive, not thrive.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Fatigue made it hard to focus on videos or dense readings. Sometimes I'd open materials and just stare at them without processing. Competing responsibilities meant I often had to do the bare minimum on the LMS and supplement with offline studying when I had clearer headspace.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd scroll through materials or watch videos on autopilot without really engaging my brain. I'd be physically present but mentally somewhere else, worrying about other things or just too tired to concentrate. Those sessions felt unproductive even though technically I was 'studying.' I learned to recognize when I was in that state and try to take a break instead, but it wasn't always possible.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, even knowing they were important. Sometimes it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start, so avoidance felt easier in the moment. Other times I'd prioritize work shifts because I needed the income immediately. I always came back to the tasks eventually, but that delay affected my completion rates. I'm not proud of it, but I understand why it happened.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd be much more active in the days leading up to a submission, downloading materials, checking requirements, and posting in discussions. Without a deadline looming, I'd sometimes let days pass without logging in. It wasn't the healthiest pattern, but the deadlines gave me structure when my motivation was low. They were like external pressure that compensated for my internal drive.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. Positive feedback motivated me to engage more and made me feel like my effort was worth it. Critical feedback sometimes made me anxious and I'd delay looking at it, which wasn't helpful. Once I got past the initial reaction, constructive feedback did help me focus my studying better. The peer support really helped me process feedback without spiraling into negativity.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to work within, which I needed. Having clear modules and deadlines helped me plan my limited time. But when the structure was too rigid or didn't account for students with different circumstances, it felt restrictive. I appreciated when there was some flexibility built in because my life doesn't always fit neatly into academic schedules. The structure was helpful but not always accommodating.
Q19: The discussion forums made engagement easier because they connected me with other students and made the course feel less isolating. Having materials available for download was crucial for my situation. What made it harder was when content was only available as streaming video with no transcript or when activities required being online at specific times. Those design choices didn't work well for someone with my resources and schedule.
Q20: I think students might be exploring, trying to find what they need, or feeling overwhelmed by the amount of content. They could be reading and watching but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. Sometimes high activity is actually a sign of confusion, not engagement. Or maybe they're dealing with things outside of school that make it hard to follow through, even when they're putting in effort on the platform.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me. I performed reasonably well despite not having the highest LMS activity because I did so much learning offline. Efficient studying and good exam preparation don't always require constant platform presence. Some students might be using external resources, studying with peers, or just processing information in ways that don't generate clicks. Activity metrics don't capture the full picture of learning.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when students are active, what they access, and how often they participate in discussions. That's useful data. But logs miss the quality of engagement, the thinking happening offline, the conversations with peers outside the platform, and the personal circumstances affecting students. They can't capture whether someone is genuinely learning or just going through the motions. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just about showing up or clicking through materials. Real engagement for me involves asking questions, making connections to other things I know, discussing ideas with peers, and feeling like the material matters. It's an internal state that doesn't always have external indicators. I can be deeply engaged while sitting quietly with a notebook.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience in bouncing back from difficult weeks, or the emotional support I got from peer connections that kept me going. It wouldn't capture the hours I spent studying on buses or during work breaks, or how I'd explain concepts to myself while doing dishes. My growth as a learner, my determination despite limited resources, and the genuine happiness I felt when things clicked, none of that shows up in data. The human side of my education is invisible to the system.
